what_it_does: Defines the ui-contract entry for Claude Code hooks: the lifecycle-automation handlers declared in JSON settings, plugin hooks.json, or skill/agent frontmatter. It documents the data model (event catalog, matcher grammar, handler schema), the read-only /hooks menu, and planned add/update/remove operations, while explicitly stating that the company exposes no hook editor or write endpoint today.
observations: Hooks are not addressable records; identity is the tuple (settings-source, hook-event, matcher-group-index, handler-index) and there is no hook:// scheme.
Configuration tree is hooks → event → matcher groups → handlers; handlers are one of command, http, mcp_tool, prompt, or agent.
All company operations (hooks.get, hooks.act, hooks.event-catalog) are marked planned; the real read path is the native /hooks TUI menu and the real write path is direct JSON file edit.
Includes JSON schemas for both the hooks.config representation and the planned hooks.act request.
Provides a closed event catalog grouped by cadence, plus per-event matcher semantics and whether exit code 2 can block.
Command hooks use exit codes as the decision channel: 0 success, 2 blocking error, other non-zero non-blocking except WorktreeCreate aborts on any non-zero.
Common handler fields include if, timeout, statusMessage, and once; once is only honored in skill frontmatter.
disableAllHooks is a settings-level boolean that respects the managed-settings hierarchy.
Relates to permission, extensions, mcp-servers, and output-style resources.
A refocus note explains that previously cited command-wrapper endpoints were removed and the capability is now reached by driving a real Claude Code session.
Error vocabulary is limited to hooks.unknown-event and hooks.not-exposed, both teaching the native recovery path.
standouts: The file is unusually honest about the bridge gap: it documents a full UI contract for a capability the company does not yet expose.
Hooks are framed as configuration entries inside settings files, not as first-class addressable resources.
Detailed exit-code/blockability semantics are specified as a safety-critical part of the contract.
hooks.act is a planned settings-writer with no existing runtime binding.
conventions: Follows the contract-entry frontmatter pattern: type, resource, summary, schemes, status, relates-to.
Uses contract:schema, contract:op, contract:example, and contract:error fenced blocks.
Heavily cross-references canonical Claude Code docs and internal wiki-style links.
Adopts a neutral, self-aware tone that names missing implementation rather than hiding it.
Breaks typical API-contract convention by specifying native CLI/TUI behavior and a 501-equivalent error for a non-existent endpoint.
concerns: Large planned surface may drift as the native Claude Code hooks reference evolves.
hooks.act request schema assumes a scope enum and settings-writer that do not yet exist, potentially over-specifying.
Tables mix planned and current reality in ways that could be misread as implemented.
No explicit mechanism is given for resolving duplicate hooks across the scope hierarchy.
notes: A deliberately incomplete contract: it fully specifies the Claude Code hooks data model and a future editor, but repeatedly marks every company operation as planned because no hook-management endpoint exists yet.
questions: What are the exact filesystem paths and formats for each scope (user, project, local, managed)?
How does the UI editor merge or prioritize hooks when the same event/matcher/handler appears in multiple scopes?
What distinguishes managed-marketplace plugin hooks from other plugin hooks?
How are skill/agent frontmatter hooks merged with settings-file hooks?
How will the event catalog be versioned or synchronized with Claude Code releases?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches — the stated purpose is to contract the native data model a UI editor would render, and the file does exactly that while also documenting the missing company endpoint and native recovery paths.
intention_signals: To give a UI editor a complete, source-linked target to build toward for hook management, while preventing the system from pretending the endpoint already exists; it names the missing bridge so future work can close it.
novelty: It documents a planned, absent feature by fully specifying the native data model, operations, and a 501-style error with native recovery instructions — uncommon for a contract entry.
contribution: A comprehensive, reference-backed specification of the Claude Code hooks data model, event catalog, matcher grammar, handler schema, and planned editor semantics, plus an honest not-exposed error vocabulary that any fused system would keep as the baseline for future hook-editor work.
summary_for_embedding: A ui-contract entry that documents the Claude Code hooks lifecycle-automation data model, event catalog, handler schema, and planned read/write operations, explicitly noting that the company currently exposes no hook editor or endpoint.
intention_for_embedding: To provide a UI-facing contract and implementation target for a future hook editor while transparently recording that the underlying company endpoint does not yet exist.
